title-group.article-title: Exploring the association between the post-pandemic period and psychological problems among university students in Wuhan: a cross-sectional analysis

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/00e4hrk88grid.412787.f0000 0000 9868 173XBrain Science and Advanced Technology Institute, Wuhan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan,, China
2https://ror.org/00p991c53grid.33199.310000 0004 0368 7223Hubei, Cancer Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, China
3https://ror.org/04jcykh16grid.433800.c0000 0000 8775 1413International College, Wuchang Institute of Technology, Wuhan, China
4https://ror.org/00e4hrk88grid.412787.f0000 0000 9868 173XGeriatric Hospital Affiliated to Wuhan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, China
5https://ror.org/00e4hrk88grid.412787.f0000 0000 9868 173XSchool of Public Health, Wuhan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, China

You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: Evidence demonstrates that Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection influences mental health through biological, psychological, and social mechanisms. In the post-pandemic period, defined as the time following the easing of major public health restrictions and the return to normalcy in daily life, university students represent a vulnerable population, experiencing a high prevalence of psychological problems due to the lingering influence of the pandemic on daily life and the emergence of long-term health consequences. There is an urgent need to understand these mental health symptoms and quantify their associated factors.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A questionnaire was designed to investigate the mental health symptoms of anxiety, depression, and insomnia among university students in Wuhan, focusing on four key domains: individual, family, school, and pandemic. Logistic regression was employed to analyze the related factors and conduct attribution analysis.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Of the 2,668 eligible participants, 2,112 (79.2%) self-reported Omicron infection, and 352 (16.7%) experienced Long-COVID. The prevalence of moderate-to-severe depression was 18.2% (95% CI: 16.8%-19.7%), anxiety was 8.9% (95% CI: 7.8% − 10.0%) and insomnia was 31.4% (95% CI: 29.6%-33.1%). Moderate-to-severe psychological symptoms among university students were associated with ten factors across the four key domains, with total weighted Population Attributable Fractions (PAFs) accounting for 60.1% of depression, 40.8% of anxiety, and 61.6% of insomnia. Individual factors had the highest PAFs, contributing 31.2% to depression, 23.8% to anxiety, and 25.1% to insomnia, followed by pandemic-related PAFs of 17.7% ,10.3%, and 21.9% for depression, anxiety, and insomnia, respectively.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The psychological challenges confronting university students in Wuhan are alarmingly severe, with individual factors superimposed on the pandemic caused by SARS-CoV2 being the primary contributors. We propose targeted intervention measures: strengthening psychological counseling services, increasing communication and exchange to enhance students’ psychological resilience, raising awareness about mental health and lifestyle, alleviating students’ fear of sequelae, assisting university students with academic planning and career guidance, cultivating adaptability, and promoting overall physical and mental health.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12889-025-25030-y.

funding-source.institution: National Natural Science Foundation of China

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2025

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The outbreak of Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV2) has posed significant challenges to public health and has profoundly affected mental health worldwide [1]. The COVID-19 pandemic has disrupted daily life in unprecedented ways [2], leading to a surge in stress, anxiety, and depression across diverse populations through biological, psychological, and social mechanisms [3, 4]. Among these populations, university students have emerged as particularly vulnerable to these mental health challenges.

COVID-19 has direct influences on individuals’ well-being, such as significantly impaired lung diffusion capacity and thoracic imaging abnormalities [5], which can have long-term consequences. Psychologically, the pandemic has introduced a series of challenges, including prolonged isolation, which strained social relationships, academic disruptions that heightened stress levels, and uncertainty about the future, exacerbating feelings of loneliness and anxiety [6–8]. These psychological influences are closely intertwined with the biological effects; for example, the stress caused by disrupted social and academic life may exacerbate the physical recovery process for those who have been infected.

The post-pandemic period is defined as the time following the easing of major public health restrictions and the return to normalcy in daily life. It is characterized by the emergence of “Long-COVID” or “post-COVID” conditions, which include neurologic symptoms such as cognitive impairment and fatigue [9–12] as well as psychological distress, including anxiety and depression [12–14]. Systematic reviews and meta-analyses have shown that among various populations, university students exhibit the highest prevalence of depression and insomnia [15, 16]. University students are one of the vulnerable groups to factors related to the COVID-19 pandemic [15], as their mental health is influenced by infection status, occupation-related stressors (e.g., part-time jobs or internships during the pandemic), close contact with infected individuals, residence in high-risk areas, high exposure risk, and their own psychological states. These factors interact in complex ways, further deteriorating their mental health.

Despite the growing awareness of the mental health issues among university students during the pandemic, significant research gaps remain. While previous studies have identified various factors affecting their mental health, few have comprehensively quantified the prevalence of psychological symptoms. The lack of quantification hinders the development of targeted and effective interventions. Additionally, the relationship between Long-COVID symptoms and psychological distress has not been fully explored in the context of university students.

Given these gaps, this study aims to investigate the mental health symptoms and their associated factors among university students in Wuhan, China, during the post-pandemic period. Specifically, we aim to: (1) Quantify the prevalence of moderate-to-severe anxiety, depression, and insomnia in this population. (2) Identify the individual, family, school, and pandemic-related factors that significantly contribute to these mental health challenges. (3) Estimate the population attributable fractions (PAFs) of these factors to determine their relative influence on mental health outcomes. By achieving these objectives, this research seeks to provide a comprehensive understanding of the intricate relationships between these variables. The findings of this research are expected to provide valuable insights for public health policy-making and to facilitate the development of targeted interventions and support systems, which are essential for proactively addressing the mental health challenges faced by university students.

sec.title: Study population
sec.p: This cross-sectional study (Fig. 1) was conducted among students from three categories of universities in Wuhan: National Key Comprehensive Universities (k-NU, n = 7), Provincial Key Comprehensive Universities (k-PU, n = 20), and General Private Comprehensive Universities (g-PU, n = 26). The numbers in parentheses indicate the total number of universities in each category. The study was conducted from April 24 to 30, 2023. Students were sampled proportionally based on the university’s enrollment size and program offerings. Participants registered for the anonymous online survey via email, completed it, and submitted their responses within one week of agreeing to participate. The inclusion criteria were (1) current enrollment and (2) full-time student status. The exclusion criteria were (1) international students, (2) students diagnosed with a serious mental disorder by a healthcare provider, and (3) students participating in an externship. Respondents provided written informed consent online, which included assurances of anonymity and confidentiality, before completing the questionnaire. The survey took eight minutes to complete.

caption.p: Flowchart of study. One of each of the three types of comprehensive universities located in Wuhan was randomly selected, and a proportional sample was selected to obtain 2,668 eligible participants

sec.title: Data collection
sec.p: The survey encompassed evaluations of participants’ self-reported COVID-19 infection status and experiences with Long-COVID symptoms, detailed in Supplementary Material 1. Participants were queried about their infection history with COVID-19, including confirmation and the timing of the illness. To assess Long-COVID symptoms, a comprehensive questionnaire was administered, covering demographic details, pre-existing psychological conditions, family environment, and COVID-19 infection status. A standardized symptom checklist was utilized to identify symptoms that persisted or appeared more than two months post-infection, without alternative explanations. This checklist encompasses symptoms affecting the respiratory, neurological, digestive, cardiovascular, and endocrine systems, as well as musculoskeletal and sensory disturbances. Additionally, standardized psychological assessment tools were incorporated: the Chinese version of the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) [17] to measure depressive symptom severity; the Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 (GAD-7) [18], also in Chinese, to gauge anxiety symptom severity; and the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI) [19] in Chinese to assess the severity of insomnia.

sec.title: Calculation of PAFs
sec.p: PAFs were used to estimate the proportion of mental health outcomes attributable to specific risk factors. PAFs were calculated using the following formula: PAF = P × (RR – 1)/(1 + P × [RR – 1]), where p is the proportion of individuals exposed to the risk factor and RR is the relative risk. In this study, PAFs were calculated with adjustments for potential confounding variables, including gender, age, university type, and family income, using multivariate regression analysis to ensure the accuracy of the results.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Statistical analyses were conducted using SPSS statistical software version 26 (SPSS, IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA). Participant characteristics were described in terms of their distribution. Chi-square tests assessed the prevalence of anxiety, depression, and insomnia in the total sample. Logistic regression models were used to explore possible correlates of the three types of mental health symptoms. Adjusted odds ratios (aOR) and 95% confidence interval (CIs) were determined. Furthermore, following principal component analysis, the aORs were used to calculate PAFs for depression, anxiety, and insomnia, as along with weighted PAFs [20, 21] (Supplementary Material 2), with the significance level set at P < 0.05.

sec.title: Characteristics of participants
sec.p: A total of 2,668 eligible participants were recruited from the three different universities in Wuhan. The mean age of the participants was 19.37 (SD = 1.5) years, and of which 1,640 (61.5%) were female and 231 (8.7%) were from ethnic minorities. 909 (34.1%) were from g-PU; regarding majors, 381 (14.3%) majored in Literature/History/Philosophy, 774 (29.0%) in Management/Education, 957 (35.9%) in Science/Engineering, and 556 (20.8%) in Medicine. Personality traits revealed that 800 (30.0%) showed agreeableness, 715 (26.8%) extroversion, 608 (22.8%) neuroticism, 327 (12.3%) openness, and 218 (8.2%) conscientiousness. Educational background indicated that 939 (35.2%) participants’ mothers had 7–9 years of education, and 1,489 (55.8%) had an annual family income of less than 100,000 CNY (defined as low income). Notably, 2,183 (81.8%) students reported experiencing more than one psychological problem in the past five years. The influence of the pandemic on social interactions was reported by 827 (31.0%) of participants. The prevalence of moderate-to-severe symptoms was 18.2% for depression, 8.9% for anxiety, and 31.4% for insomnia (Table 1 and Table S1).

tr.td: Type of psychological distress have experienced past 5 years

table-wrap-foot.p: Abbreviation: k-NU Key National Universities, k-PU Key Provincial Universities, g-PU General Private Universities, LHP Literature/History/Philosophy, ME Management/Education, SE Science/Engineering, M Medicine

sec.title: Long-COVID symptoms
sec.p: Among the 2,668 university students included in the analysis, 2,112 (79.2%) reported being infected with the Omicron variant, and 352 (16.7%) experienced Long-COVID symptoms (Table S2). The prevalence of Long-COVID symptoms was the highest among freshman and showed significant associations with sex and age. Long-COVID symptoms were categorized into four main groups: respiratory, neurological, sensory, and reproductive sequelae. The distribution of these symptoms is summarized in Table S2. Respiratory symptoms were reported by 125 students (5.9%), with cough being the most common symptom (4.7%). Neurological symptoms were the most prevalent, affecting 211 students (10.0%) and included fatigue (6.4%) and drowsiness (6.2%). Sensory disturbances were reported by 130 students (6.2%), including decreased vision (2.9%) and loss of smell (2.2%) being the primary issues. Reproductive sequelae were noted in 3.1% of the students, with irregular menstruation reported by 4.1% of females.

The prevalence of Long-COVID symptoms varied significantly across demographic groups. Freshman students exhibited higher rates of all symptom categories compared to other academic years (p < 0.05). Female students were more likely to report sensory disturbances and reproductive sequelae, while neurological symptoms were more prevalent among students aged 18–20 years (p < 0.05). These findings suggest that demographic factors, particularly academic year, sex, and age, play a significant role in the manifestation of Long-COVID symptoms among university students.

While demographic variables influenced the prevalence of Long-COVID symptoms, individual factors such as personality traits and past psychological distress had the greatest influence on mental health outcomes. Specifically, students with a history of psychological distress or specific personality traits (e.g., neuroticism) were more likely to experience severe mental health symptoms, independent of their Long-COVID status. This highlights the importance of considering both demographic and individual factors when assessing the mental health needs of university students in the post-pandemic period.

sec.title: Prevalence and associated factors associated with anxiety, depression, and insomnia
sec.p: The prevalence of self-reported moderate-to-severe mental health symptoms among participants was 18.2% (95% CI: 16.8% − 19.7%) for depression, 8.9% (95% CI: 7.8% − 10.0%) for anxiety, and 31.4% (95% CI: 29.6% − 33.1%) for insomnia. We used multivariate logistic regression to investigate the factors associated with these symptoms among university students, reporting aORs and 95% CIs (Table S3, Table S4, and Fig. 2).

caption.p: Forest plot of factors associated with moderate-to-severe psychological symptoms analyzed by multiple logistic regression. Moderate-to-severe depression, anxiety, and insomnia psychological symptoms in university students are primarily associated with 4 key domains 10 factors, including school-related factors (school level, major, and grade), individual-related factors (personality traits and experience of psychological problems in the past 5 years), family-related factors (mother's education level and annual family income), and pandemic-related factors (symptoms of long-COVID, the influence on quality of life, and worry about recurrent infections). Abbreviation: k-NU, Key National Universities; k-PU, Key Provincial Universities; g-PU, General Private Universities; LHP, Literature/History/Philosophy; ME, Management/Education; SE, Science/Engineering; M, Medicine. CI, confidence interval

sec.p: Females experienced less insomnia than males (aOR = 0.8, 95% CI: 0.7 −1.0, p = 0.043); while students in g-PU reported lower anxiety (aOR = 0.5, 95% CI: 0.3–0.9, p = 0.012). The incidence of depression (aOR = 0.5, 95% CI: 0.4–0.8, p = 0.004) and anxiety (aOR = 0.5, 95% CI: 0.3–0.9, p = 0.014) was lower in students majoring in Management/Education compared to those majoring in Literature/History/Philosophy. Sophomores (aOR = 1.5, 95% CI: 1.1–2.1, p = 0.020) were more likely to suffer from depression than freshmen, while juniors (aOR = 2.2, 95% CI: 1.0–4.6, p = 0.044) had a higher likelihood of experiencing anxiety than freshmen.

Compared to students with neuroticism, those with other personality traits exhibited significantly lower prevalence of psychological problems with agreeableness correlating with lower rates of depression (aOR = 0.3, 95% CI: 0.2–0.4), anxiety (aOR = 0.2, 95% CI: 0.1–0.3,) and insomnia (aOR = 0.4, 95% CI: 0.3–0.6). Students who experienced psychological distress in the past five years were at higher risk than those who did not, with those experiencing four or more episodes of psychological distress exhibiting increased prevalence of depression (aOR = 17.1, 95% CI: 9.2–31.8), anxiety (aOR = 13.5, 95% CI: 5.3–34.7), and insomnia (aOR = 8.9, 95% CI: 6.1–12.9) (all p < 0. 001).

The father’s level of education had less of an influence on students’ psychological problems; however, as the mother’s level of education increased, the prevalence of psychological issue also increased. Students with mothers who had more than 12 years of education were more likely to suffer from depression (aOR = 1.8, 95% CI: 1.1–2.8, p = 0.016) than those with mothers who had 9 years or less of education. Students with an annual family income of 100,000–300,000 CNY (defined as a medium income) reported less insomnia (aOR = 0.8, 95% CI: 0.6–1.0, p = 0.025) than their peers. Among pandemic-related factors, the influence of the pandemic on life and concerns had a limited association on psychological symptoms, with a significant increase in anxiety only among students who believed the pandemic had an influence on their social interactions (aOR = 1.6, 95% CI: 1.2–2.3, p = 0.05). Long-COVID symptoms, however, significantly associated with students’ psychological problems. Long-COVID respiratory, neurological, sensory disturbances, reproductive, and poor appetite increased the prevalence of moderate-to-severe psychological problems. Notably, the greatest odds were long-COVID neurological symptoms in depression (aOR = 2.1, 95% CI: 1.4–3.1, p < 0.001); poor appetite on anxiety (aOR = 2.8, 95% CI: 1.4–5.6, p = 0.003); and reproductive symptoms on insomnia (aOR = 2.0, 95% CI: 1.1–3.5, p = 0.020).

sec.title: PAFs of all-factors and pandemic-related factors on anxiety, depression, and insomnia
sec.p: We estimated the PAFs of four key domains (individual, family, school, and pandemic) after performing a principal component analysis (Table S5), resulting in ten factors for the three mental health symptoms based on the aORs among Omicron-infected students (Table S6 and Table S7). The proportions of symptoms attributed to these factors were 60.1% (95% CI: 58.0–62.2) depression, 40.8% (95% CI: 38.7–42.9) anxiety and 61.6% (95% CI: 59.5–63.6) for insomnia. Among these, two individual factors - personality traits and experience of psychological problems in the past five years were the most significant risk factors, contributing overall weighted PAFs of 23.8% (95% CI: 22.0–25.7) and 7.4% (95% CI: 6.3–8.5) for depression; 17.5% (95% CI: 15.9–19.2) and 6.3% (95% CI: 5.2–7.3) for anxiety, and 20.0% (95% CI: 18.3–21.7) and 5.1% (95% CI: 4.1–6.0) for insomnia. Additionally, post-pandemic related factors, including Long-COVID symptoms, the influence of the pandemic on life (daily life/social/academic) and concerns about recurrent infections and sequelae were risk factors with overall weighted PAFs of 13.5% (95% CI: 12.0–14.9), 3.3% (95% CI: 2.5–4.0) and 0.9% (95% CI: 0.5–1.2) for depression, 7.7% (95% CI: 6.5–8.8), 2.0% (95% CI: 1.4–2.6) and 0.6% (95% CI: 0.3–0.9) for anxiety, and 13.8% (95% CI: 12.4–15.3), 4.8% (95% CI: 3.9–5.8) and 3.3% (95% CI: 2.5–4.0) for insomnia. Finally, the mother’s education and annual family income were minor risk factors, contributing overall weighted PAFs of 0.4% (95% CI: 0.1–0.6) and 0.4% (95% CI: 0.2–0.7) for depression; 0.8% (95% CI: 0.4–1.2) and 0.3% (95% CI: 0.1–0.5) for anxiety; and 2.3% (95% CI: 1.6–2.9) and 4.1% (95% CI: 3.3–5.0) for insomnia respectively (Fig. 3).

caption.p: PAFs of all-factors on anxiety, depression and insomnia. Moderate-to-severe psychological symptoms in university students were linked to 10 factors spanning four key domains. The combined weighted population attributable fractions (PAFs) for depression, anxiety, and insomnia were 60.1%, 40.8%, and 61.6% respectively. Individual factors showed the highest PAFs at 31.2%, 23.8%, and 25.1%, followed by epidemic-related PAFs at 17.7%, 10.3%, and 21.9% respectively (The PAF calculation methodology is shown in the supplementary material 2). Abbreviation: RF, related factors; g-PU, General Private Universities; ME, Management/Education

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study investigated the psychological problems of the entire university student population during the post-pandemic period using an anonymous online survey. Quantitative analysis and logistic regression were employed to determine the associations of individual, school, family, and pandemic-related factors with anxiety, depression, and insomnia. Key findings include: (1) The prevalence of moderate-to-severe depression, anxiety, and insomnia among university students was higher than in the general population during the early stages of the pandemic but lower than during the 2022 Omicron outbreak. (2) Psychological problems were associated with maternal education level, personality traits (especially neuroticism), previous psychological distress, and Long-COVID symptoms. (3) PAFs highlight personality traits and prior psychological issues as key factors linked to mental health symptoms in university students, with Long-COVID and pandemic influences also playing significant roles.

Compared with the general population, university students exhibited higher rates of psychological problems during the early pandemic [15]. This difference can be attributed to several factors unique to this population: (1) Academic Stress: University students face unique stressors, such as academic pressure, uncertainty about future careers, and disruptions in educational plans [22]. (2) Social Isolation: Prolonged social distancing measures led to reduced connections with peers, family, and romantic partners, exacerbating feelings of loneliness and anxiety [23]. (3) Vulnerability to Pandemic-Related Stressors: The pandemic’s influence on university students was compounded by disruptions in daily routines, online learning inefficiencies, and concerns about repeated infections [24].

Personality traits, while describe an individual’s relatively stable patterns of thinking, feeling, and behaving [25], are endogenous to psychological problems and play a crucial role in intra-individual and interpersonal regulation [26].Neuroticism, a personality trait characterized by emotional instability and reactivity, significantly amplifies psychological distress during the pandemic. Individuals with high neuroticism are more susceptible to anxiety, tension, and frustration in response to stressors such as academic delays and Long-COVID symptoms [27]. This trait may predispose individuals to internalize stress, leading to increased psychological symptoms [28]. Individuals with high neuroticism are prone to relapse of previous psychological issues and distress, reflecting heightened stress sensitivity and variations in cognitive perception of mental distress. Both stress sensitivity and cognitive limitations can trigger internalizing disorders, thereby contributing to psychological problems and even suicidal ideation [28].While neuroticism exacerbates psychological distress, other personality traits may have protective influences [29]. For example, conscientiousness, characterized by self-discipline and responsibility, may buffer against stress by promoting adaptive coping strategies and resilience [26]. Future research should explore the protective roles of such traits in mitigating pandemic-related psychological problems.

Beyond individual traits, familial factors such as maternal education and genetic inheritance further shape this vulnerability. Maternal unpredictable emotions and behaviors are one of the factors increasing the risk of psychopathology in adolescents [30]. Behavioral habits formed in childhood and adolescence can affect the entire life course and mental health. Similarly, there is an association between maternal education levels and psychological problems in universities [31], which may be due to multiple factors. First, higher maternal education often leads to higher academic and developmental expectations for children, thus imposing greater psychological pressure on them [31]. Second, mothers with higher education may have demanding jobs, leaving them with less time to provide emotional support for their children [32]. Third, maternal stress and emotional fluctuations during early childhood can influence children’s personality development, making them more prone to psychological problems in adulthood [26], and such influence might be indirectly delivered through academic stress or high expectations.

Notably, heredity is likely to be a crucial element in molding a child’s emotional and behavioral makeup. Beyond establishing the biological basis for a child’s physical growth, parental genes can also shape the fundamental aspects of a child’s emotional and behavioral attributes [33]. For instance, genetic variations in neurotransmitter systems, such as the 5-HTTLPR polymorphism encoded by the SLC6A4 gene, which regulates synaptic serotonin levels, and brain structural connections like amygdala-hippocampus circuitry, may be influenced by parental genes [34]. Moreover, TEF polymorphisms have been associated with depressive symptoms in individuals with major depressive disorder (MDD) and sleep disorders [34]. These genetic factors can interact with environmental factors such as family dynamics, educational background, and parental work stress to affect psychological development [35, 36]. They can also interact with the environment through epigenetic mechanisms like DNA methylation of the glucocorticoid receptor gene (NR3C1), which modulates the HPA axis stress response. Genetic variations associated with heightened stress reactivity, such as those in the DRD2 gene, may amplify the impact of maternal emotional instability on offspring [37, 38]. This gene-environment interplay suggests that parents with specific genetic profiles may transmit both genetic risks and stress-inducing parenting styles. Thus, the relationship between maternal education and offspring psychological problems likely involves both environmental and genetic factors. Future research could use twin or family-based study designs to better understand the heritable and environmental components of this relationship.

Long-COVID sequelae, including neurological and cognitive deficits, are recognized as significant contributors to psychological problems in the later stages of the SARS-CoV2 pandemic [39]. These neurological sequelae, such as fatigue, brain fog, and sensory disturbances, can be particularly debilitating. SARS-CoV-2 may induce neuroinflammation via TLR4-mediated microglial activation, which interacts with genetic markers of immune regulation (e.g., TNF-α polymorphisms) to exacerbate depressive symptoms [40]. This biological mechanism highlights how Long-COVID symptoms significantly influence mental health by disrupting daily functioning, exacerbating feelings of helplessness, and contributing to anxiety and depression. The interplay between Long-COVID symptoms and existing psychological vulnerabilities (e.g., high neuroticism) creates a complex landscape of mental health challenges among university students.

However, our study indicates that respiratory symptoms show no significant link to insomnia (OR = 1.104, 95% CI: 0.727–1.677). This may be because their influence is minor, or it wasn’t fully captured in our sample. We examined cough and chest pain persisting 3 months post-acute phase, which might be mild or transitory, thus having limited direct influence on sleep. However, this doesn’t mean they don’t influence overall health. They may indirectly influence sleep quality by daily activities or psychological state, yet this influence could be masked by other factors or require a larger sample or longer-term follow-up to clarify. Neuropsychiatric Long-COVID symptoms also show no significant association with anxiety (OR = 1.573, 95% CI: 0.922–2.681). Anxiety is a complex emotional state influenced by multiple factors. Long-COVID’s neuropsychiatric symptoms might be just one of many factors, and their isolated influence may not significantly increase anxiety risk. Our assessment of these symptoms might not have been comprehensive, or the measurement tools might not have captured subtle anxiety changes. Given the dynamic nature of Long-COVID symptoms and their mental health influence over time, future research should conduct larger-scale, long-term follow-up studies to better understand the dynamic relationships between these symptoms.

The term “Long-COVID” highlights that symptoms and suffering can persist beyond the acute phase of infection [41, 42], and has been recognized as a significant long-term public health challenge [43]. Despite involving multiple symptoms across various organ systems, Long-COVID is most prominently characterized by neurological and psychiatric symptoms, which are more common and debilitating in the long-term following infection [44]. Recent studies have revealed multiple mechanisms through which SARS-CoV2 infection causes or exacerbates persistent fatigue and neuropsychological deficits. These mechanisms include, but are not limited to, direct viral encephalitis, neuroinflammation [45–47], hypoxia, cerebrovascular disease [48, 49], endothelial dysfunction [50], hyperinflammatory response, autoimmunity, latent viral reactivation [51], multiorgan pathology, and dysfunction of autonomic nervous system, which may act synergistically [52]. Although these mechanisms are not yet fully defined, our study reinforces that the sequelae of Long-COVID, particularly those related to neurological conditions, are significant contributors to psychological problems.

We identified the overlap of Long-COVID sequelae based on individual personality traits and previous experiences of psychological issues as a major cause of current psychological problems among university students. We estimated the proportion of mental health consequences attributable to ten risk factors, highlighting those linked to individuals and pandemic-related issues as potential focal points for preventing psychological problems among university students. Our exploration, along with findings from other studies, has identified several causes and manifestations of psychological problems among university students, including: (1) Neurocognitive sequelae of learning difficulties, such as fatigue, brain fog, dizziness, headache, and memory and attention deficits [53]; (2) Increased fear related to continued transmission, severe consequences, and the risk of repeated COVID-19 infection in the population [54]; (3) Reduced connections with family, friends, peers and romantic partners due to social distancing measures [55]; (4) Heightened academic stress resulting from class suspensions, decreased online class efficiency, postponed exams, and disrupted academic planning; and (5) Socioeconomic deterioration and employment instability contributing to increased job-related stress [56]. In the post-pandemic period, biological, psychological, and social factors intersect to produce psychological symptoms among university students. Notably, the specific neuropathology associated with long-term COVID sequelae has been shown to incrementally exacerbate the internalization of stress [57].

While many studies suggest that psychological problems may improve as the pandemic recedes, our survey indicates a decrease in the prevalence of depression and anxiety but a doubling in the prevalence of insomnia. This observation underscores the ongoing challenges related to psychological issues, particularly as new virus strains emerge, keeping the risk of infection alive. Therefore, addressing the prevention and intervention of psychological problems among university students remain a critical public health concern. We recommend the following actions: (1) Timely monitoring: Systematic monitoring of psychological issues among university students across various regions, time points, and types of institutions will enhance our understanding of the dynamic effects of biological, psychological, and social mechanisms related to the pandemic on mental health. (2) Timely intervention: Strengthening education on COVID-19 prevention and control can alleviate psychological burdens; enhancing communication can reduce feelings of loneliness and fear; improving psychological counseling services facilitates early detection and treatment of psychological issues; and disseminating information on lifestyle and health care can aid in recovery from post-infection challenges and mitigate fear. (3) Social support: Family, school, and community assistance in career planning and employment guidance can help alleviate anxiety stemming from uncertainty about the future. (4) Long-term follow-up measures: More extensive and prolonged longitudinal studies are necessary in the later stages of the pandemic, given the enduring effects of COVID-19 on global politics, economy, and culture, as well as all individual aspects of daily life.

In the meantime, individuals with high neuroticism are more prone to develop psychological problems post-pandemic. To tackle this, a comprehensive approach at both personal and family levels is needed. Personally, students can do self-reflection by journaling to spot emotional triggers, learn relaxation skills like deep breathing and progressive muscle relaxation to ease stress and anxiety, and maintain a healthy lifestyle with regular exercise, balanced diet, and enough sleep for emotional stability. They should also actively make friends and use university mental health resources. Familially, parents need to actively listen to their children, create a caring home environment, model healthy emotional responses to boost children’s emotional resilience, and watch for any psychological symptoms in the children, seeking professional help when needed. In summary, it’s vital for both individuals and families to work together to protect the mental health of university students with neuroticism.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This study provides insights into the influence of SARS-CoV2 on the mental health of university students but has several limitations. Firstly, the sample was drawn from only three universities in Wuhan, which may lead to sampling bias and limit the generalization of the findings. However, the sample’s representativeness was enhanced by including diverse types of universities and employing random selection. While the Wuhan sample specifically captures the unique stressors faced in the pandemic epicenter, future studies should incorporate multi-regional cohorts to further validate these results. Second, reliance on self-reported measures introduces potential biases and inaccuracies. Future research should consider alternative validation strategies, such as clinical assessments or multi-method approaches, to improve the reliability and validity of findings. Third, the cross-sectional design limits our ability to establish causal relationships between variables and outcomes. Longitudinal studies are needed to clarify these associations. Lastly, the exclusion of international students, those with severe mental disorders, and internal students may limit the applicability of our findings to these groups. Future studies should consider including these populations to provide a more comprehensive understanding of mental health among university students.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The results of this study highlight the significant mental health challenges faced by university students in the post-pandemic period. We found elevated rates of symptoms of depression, anxiety, and insomnia, with individual and pandemic-related factors particularly contributing to these mental health issues. Future research should further explore the long-term effects and underlying mechanisms of the pandemic on university students’ mental health, as well as develop comprehensive and effective intervention strategies to ensure they receive necessary attention and support.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.
Zhichao He, Shengzhong Yi and Huanhua Zhou are joint first authors.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors wish to acknowledge all participants and investigators in the study.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: G.C. drafted the initial manuscript. S.Y. and D.L. reviewed the study design and revised the initial draft. Z.H. performed data analysis and created the tables and figures. H.Z., F.H., Q.N., D.S., X.L., J.W., J.Z., J.L., Y.L., L.X., M.OY., Y.M., and D.Z. conducted the population survey and collected data. D.L. supervised the study and revised the manuscript. All authors contributed to the manuscript and approved the final version.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81870901), and the Hubei Teaching Program (No. 2020355).

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The datasets used and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: The study was carried out following the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki. The medical Ethics committee of Wuhan University of Science and Technology School approved the study. All participants gave informed consent prior to the survey.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
